text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 13 April 2024 

Dr Amelia Clarke
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Amelia Clarke
Consultant Neurologist,  

Peter Wallace, DOB: 14-03-1957, Hospital No: H739204 NHS No. 9075623148  
12 Brookside Close, London, SE15 2LR

Thank you for asking me to review this newly retired teacher who lives alone and maintains a regular daily routine with reduced social contact since leaving work. He attended today unaccompanied. He described being generally well organised, keeping fixed sleeping and mealtime hours, and going for short walks locally. He does not drive. He reports drinking minimal alcohol and no recreational drug use. He keeps a diary of events and brings this to clinic. 

He explained that since discharge from hospital he has experienced 2 seizures, the last one being on 11 February. He was unable to identify a clear precipitant, though he wonders whether poorer sleep during a spell of winter colds may have contributed. There have been no clear daytime focal warnings recorded in his diary, and he does not describe tongue biting or urinary incontinence; recovery was reported to be gradual over several hours with tiredness and headache. There is no witnessed collateral history available as he lives alone. He has noticed increased anxiety about being out in public in case a further event occurs. 

Current medication is levetiracetam 1 g twice daily, which he has been taking since discharge. He uses a pill organiser and denies missed doses. No rash or gastrointestinal side effects. He mentions low mood at times, which he attributes to social isolation and worry about further events rather than a persistent change in personality. Sleep is described as reasonable, with occasional early morning waking. 

On examination today he was alert, oriented and cooperative. Speech was fluent, cranial nerves intact, no lateralising motor or sensory deficits, coordination normal and gait steady. No features to suggest an intercurrent systemic illness. 

Given the limited collateral information and the absence of a consistent warning pattern, I have advised continued diary keeping and would favour obtaining further objective data. I have requested an outpatient EEG and updated MRI brain (previous imaging was reported during his admission but we do not yet have the images). I have also arranged routine blood tests including FBC, U&E, LFTs and vitamin D. He is agreeable to this plan. We discussed the DVLA guidance and he is aware of current restrictions. 

From a wellbeing perspective, he reports reduced social contact since retirement and would welcome information about community support. I have signposted him to local peer support groups and suggested brief input from our epilepsy specialist nurse to reinforce safety advice at home, particularly around bathing and cooking. 

I will review him after the EEG and MRI are available, or sooner if there are further events or concerns. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: since discharge from hospital he has experienced 2 seizures, the last one being on 11 February
He explained that since discharge from hospital he has experienced 2 seizures, the last one being on 11 February

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states: "since discharge from hospital he has experienced 2 seizures, the last one being on 11 February." Clinic date is 13 April 2024. There is no explicit timeframe for the two seizures other than 'since discharge', and the discharge date is not provided. Without a defined period, we cannot convert '2 seizures since discharge' into a per day/week/month/year rate or confirm 6 months seizure freedom (last seizure ~2 months prior). Therefore, the specific frequency cannot be determined from the provided information.
